author.name: BostnMike

weeks.title: The Week That Was
weeks.dek: A week of fresh faces, fresh bruises, and one very productive trip to the cash cage by NASA Al.
weeks.html:
The TLPT site tells you exactly what kind of circus this is: The League. The Players. The Tilt. The homepage promises the stats never forget, the standings page lets you sort the damage in public, and the dashboard functions like an audit conducted by someone who already doesn’t trust you. In other words, this league did not merely have a week. It left evidence.

“This league did not merely have a week. It left evidence.”

🎯 The Big Story

The biggest story was not at the very top of the profit board, where Li-Fo still sits like a man expecting interest payments. It was one rung below, where NASA Al quietly put together the best established-player heater of the week.

Profit climbed. Hits climbed. Cashes climbed. It was the kind of performance that doesn’t need chest-thumping because the spreadsheet already did the talking.

NA
Week’s Cleanest Heater
NASA Al
Profit ↑Hits ↑Cashes ↑Luck Rank ↑

Meanwhile, Mike-G showed up like he thought the room had been saving him a seat. Pat treated the ROI board like temporary housing. Kishan stepped in and immediately looked like he understood the assignment better than some returning players.

And then there was ProvidenceMike, who spent the week serving as a public service announcement. More entries, more pain, less joy. Some men run bad. Some men turn the stat sheet into a witness statement.

📈 Movers

NASA Al

The cleanest veteran rise on the table. More profit, more hits, more cashes, less nonsense. In a league built on noise, he had the rare useful week.

Mike-G

Went from blank space to immediate problem. That is not a debut. That is a disruption with a player profile.

Pat

Barely any volume, maximum efficiency, and now he owns the best ROI mark in the league. That is a very rude way to introduce yourself.

Kishan

One week, one clean impression, no wasted movement. The statistical version of showing up to a pickup game and immediately being one of the best players there.

ProvidenceMike

Took the hardest hit of the week. More entries, less joy, and a profit line that reads like a billing error.

Li-Fo

Still the profit boss, but the polish took a hit. Still rich, just slightly less smug.

Hiro

The prime numbers may still be beautiful. The results were not.

Ahmed

Added a bubble and lost ground. A very specific kind of week: active enough to care, punished enough to regret it.

BM

BostnMike had the classic commissioner’s paradox: more hits, more cashes, more bubbles, less actual joy. He now owns Most Hits (28) and Most Cashes (12), but his weekly line still came with a $25 profit drop and a slide in ROI and Power. The counting stats say “workhorse.” The efficiency stats say “maybe sit down for a minute.”

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • +$135: NASA Al’s profit swing, the strongest real heater among returning regulars.
  • 233.3% ROI: Pat now owns the top ROI mark, which is either elite efficiency or elite timing.
  • Mike-G: one week in and already statistically louder than half the room.
  • ProvidenceMike: the week’s cleanest example that volume and happiness are not the same thing.

🎉 Welcome to the League

Mike-G, Pat, and Kishan did not arrive quietly. Mike-G kicked the door open, Pat stole shelf space on the records board, and Kishan delivered the kind of efficient debut that makes regulars suddenly interested in sample-size lectures.

Which is exactly what new blood should do. Nobody joins TLPT to blend into the wallpaper.

“Nobody joins TLPT to blend into the wallpaper.”

🔥 Quick Hits

  • Mike-G needed one week to become everybody else’s problem.
  • Pat took the ROI record like he was picking up dry cleaning.
  • Kishan did not need an orientation period.
  • NASA Al looked like the only man operating with a flight plan.
  • ProvidenceMike is now on a payment plan with variance.
  • BostnMike broke records and still made the week feel expensive.
  • Li-Fo remains the king, just with a little less shine on the crown.
  • Red somehow lost money and still left fingerprints everywhere.
  • Hiro’s upside remained mostly theoretical.
  • Nitro continues to honor the low-profit arts.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

That was TLPT in a nutshell: a veteran heater, a few statistical body blows, and three newcomers who immediately made the room less comfortable. The stats never forget. This week, they also didn’t forgive.

weeks.title: The Week That Was
weeks.dek: Li-Fo tightened his grip on the league, Chris O found daylight in a hurry, and Vish turned volume into sorrow.
weeks.html:
The room did not get new blood that week. It got something worse: confirmation. Li-Fo was already sitting on top of the profit board, but by March 14 the rest of the honors page started looking like it had quietly been transferred into his name. That is not a heater anymore. That is a hostile takeover.

“By March 14, the honors page was starting to look less like a leaderboard and more like leased property.”

🎯 The Big Story

The cleanest story of the week was Li-Fo turning a strong position into full board control. Profit Leader was already his. By March 14, he had also taken Power Leader and Hit King, while the records page showed him sitting on Highest Profit, Most Hits, Most Cashes, Top True Skill, and Best Luck Index. At that point you are not just running well. You are collecting deeds.

It is also the kind of week that makes everyone else at the table start speaking in disclaimers. Small sample. Seat draw. Structure variance. Sure. Meanwhile the league still says Li-Fo, and the table is rarely interested in anyone’s emotional testimony.

LF
Week’s Biggest Board Grab
Li-Fo
Profit LeaderPower LeaderHit KingRecords Piling Up

That does not mean Li-Fo was the only one making noise. Chris O posted the week’s cleanest upward jolt, turning a red ledger into a black one in a hurry. A.l. Dave also found his way back above water, which is always encouraging for a man whose entire poker existence often feels one card away from a software warning.

And then there was Vish, who once again demonstrated that volume and happiness remain entirely separate categories. There are players who grind. There are players who donate. And then there are weeks where a player manages to look like both at once.

📈 Movers

Chris O

The week’s loudest clean heater. He went from -$85 to $100, added hits, added a cash, and generally behaved like a man who finally found the right end of the deck.

A.l. Dave

Moved from -$15 to $25 and quietly pushed the profile back into respectable territory. Not a parade, but definitely a pulse.

Li-Fo

Already rich, now somehow also collecting titles. If the league had wallpaper, his face would probably be on that too.

Cougar

Not the flashiest week, but still the kind of steady line that makes other people annoyingly aware he keeps cashing while they keep explaining themselves.

Vish

The best example of volume turning into sorrow. More action, more exposure, and the kind of profit line that reads like an apology drafted by an accountant.

Jeff T

Another week deeper in the hole, which is impressive in the wrong way and consistent in the least comforting one.

Vic

Five entries and enough bubble pain to make the week feel longer than the calendar claims it was.

Nitro

Still holding down the wrong end of the records board. There is loyalty, and then there is whatever this is.

BM

BostnMike did not lose the Bubble King belt, which is the sort of accomplishment that sounds a lot better until you remember what the award actually means.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • Li-Fo: Power Leader and Hit King by March 14, while still sitting on the profit throne.
  • Chris O: from -$85 to $100, the week’s cleanest profit surge among eligible regulars.
  • A.l. Dave: from -$15 to $25, which is not domination but is absolutely a healthier direction.
  • Vish: the clearest reminder that more entries do not automatically qualify as progress.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • Li-Fo did not just hold ground. He started annexing zip codes.
  • Chris O found $185 worth of clarity in a week, which is more than most people find in a fiscal quarter.
  • A.l. Dave got back into the black, proving that even chaos can occasionally balance a checkbook.
  • Cougar keeps cashing with the kind of steady irritation that should probably be studied.
  • Nitro remains loyal to the low-profit arts.
  • Vish once again made the case that action is not the same thing as joy.
  • Jeff T is building a résumé for hard-luck minutes nobody asked to review.
  • Vic’s bubbles were not decorative.
  • BostnMike still owned Bubble King, which is the poker equivalent of being employee of the month at the wrong factory.
  • By March 14, the honors page looked like Li-Fo had changed the locks.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was March 14: no new arrivals, no novelty act, just the old reliable cruelty of the numbers. Li-Fo tightened his grip, Chris O surged, A.l. Dave looked alive, and Vish provided the week’s cleanest case study in why volume should come with a warning label. The page remembers everything. That week, it remembered who owned the building.

weeks.title: The Week That Was
weeks.dek: ProvidenceMike finally got back to even, Red found daylight again, and Li-Fo kept looking like the landlord everyone else pays in frustration.
weeks.html:
By March 7, the room finally produced a story more satisfying than the usual top-floor rent collection. ProvidenceMike, who had spent plenty of prior weeks making the spreadsheet look like a personal grievance document, finally dragged himself back to even. In TLPT terms, that counts as both a recovery and a small miracle.

“ProvidenceMike finally broke even. The room responded by acting like this was a public holiday.”

🎯 The Big Story

The cleanest week-over-week story belonged to ProvidenceMike. Profit climbed from -$60 to $0, hits rose from 12 to 14, placements moved from 4 to 5, and for one shining moment the numbers stopped reading like a mailed threat. He did not take over the room. He just finally stopped actively donating to it, which is progress all the same.

That did not mean the skyline changed ownership. Li-Fo still held the money board exactly where he had it before, sitting on $435 profit and continuing to make everyone else’s long-term plans feel like lease agreements. ProvidenceMike won the week’s redemption story. Li-Fo still owned the property line.

PM
Week’s Best Recovery
ProvidenceMike
Profit ↑Hits ↑Placements ↑Finally Even

The undercard had plenty of motion too. Red climbed back into the black after looking very much dead the week before, which is exactly the kind of rebound that makes the room suspicious instead of supportive. Hiro, meanwhile, kept doing the grinder thing where the attendance remains admirable and the emotional return on investment remains offensive.

And because the room cannot let a clean redemption story stand without a bureaucratic counterweight, BostnMike once again handled the self-own lane himself. Still visible. Still decorated. Still somehow making less money while doing more things. Some traditions do not need rewriting.

📈 Movers

ProvidenceMike

Finally found daylight. The week’s cleanest recovery and the first snapshot in a while that did not read like an invoice.

Red

Back from the dead and back in the black, which is not always the same thing in this league but happened to be both this time.

Li-Fo

Not the week’s loudest mover, just the room’s permanent financial address. The money board still begins with him.

The Concept of Relief

ProvidenceMike briefly made it feel like a real thing.

BostnMike

Still highly visible, still holding belts, still making less money than the volume suggests a commissioner should be able to negotiate.

Hiro

More work, less joy, and another week where the grind looked nobler than the payout.

Nitro

Still serving as the room’s permanent installation on low-profit optimism and mathematically hostile outcomes.

The Bubble Economy

Still doing strong business, mostly because one man keeps refusing to let it cool off.

BM

BostnMike has now achieved the rare double-major in public visibility and private disappointment. He still held the belts, still took up space on the stat wall, and still found a way to make less money doing it. That is not a slump. That is brand consistency.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • ProvidenceMike: from -$60 to $0, with hits rising from 12 to 14 and placements from 4 to 5 — the week’s cleanest recovery.
  • Red: from -$100 to $60, which is the kind of rebound that usually arrives with a shrug and a suspicious grin.
  • Li-Fo: still sitting at $435 profit, because apparently stability at the top is just another way to make the room miserable.
  • BostnMike: still heavily involved, still heavily accessorized, and somehow thirty dollars poorer for the effort.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • ProvidenceMike finally reached break-even, which in this room qualifies as both a recovery and a diplomatic event.
  • Red bounced back hard enough to remind everyone that bad weeks are rentals, not ownership stakes.
  • Li-Fo remains the money board’s permanent landlord and occasional mood suppressor.
  • Hiro continues to grind in the exact tone of a man filing complaints nobody plans to answer.
  • Nitro is still the room’s most committed ambassador to the low-profit arts.
  • BostnMike kept the belts warm and the returns weird.
  • No major New Blood storyline this week, just the same cast rearranging the suffering.
  • The room briefly produced hope, then immediately tried to tax it.
  • The spreadsheet still has no sentiment setting and no patience for spin.
  • By March 7, the skyline had not moved. The middle of the room finally had.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was March 7: ProvidenceMike finally got back to even, Red climbed out of the hole, Li-Fo stayed rich enough to make everyone else’s progress feel conditional, and BostnMike once again demonstrated that activity and profitability are only casual acquaintances. It was a recovery week, at least for some. The receipts, naturally, remain permanent.

weeks.title: The Week That Was
weeks.dek: The commish got hot, Li-Fo got richer, and the rest of the room was left to decide whether this counted as inspiration or just another insult.
weeks.html:
By February 27, the room finally had to sit through the one thing it never really enjoys: the commissioner being right about himself for at least one week. BostnMike posted the kind of line he has been advertising in committee meetings all year — more entries, more hits, more placements, and an actual meaningful jump in profit. Of course, because TLPT is constitutionally incapable of leaving a clean story alone, Li-Fo still got richer anyway.

“The commish got hot. Li-Fo got richer.”

🎯 The Big Story

The week’s loudest surge belonged to BostnMike. Profit jumped from $240 to $390, hits climbed from 19 to 23, placements rose from 8 to 10, and for once the spreadsheet looked less like an internal audit and more like a campaign ad. It was a genuine heater — the kind of week that finally gives a player the receipts he has been speaking as if he already owned.

That still did not make him the richest man in the room. Li-Fo handled that part himself. Profit rose from $400 to $435, cashes climbed from 10 to 11, and the larger money skyline remained exactly where it has been for weeks: under his name. So yes, the commish had the week. Li-Fo still had the deed.

BM
Week’s Loudest Heater
BostnMike
Profit ↑Hits ↑Placements ↑Finally Useful

There were other real storylines underneath that top billing. Providencemike found something resembling a pulse, cutting the losses and finally producing a line that looked less like a warning label. On the other side, Red had the week’s ugliest collapse, going from comfortably positive to deeply underwater in one extremely efficient act of self-sabotage. Hiro also cooled all the way off, reminding everyone that form is temporary and humiliation is eager.

📈 Movers

BostnMike

The week’s clear heater. Bigger profit, more hits, more placements, and a line that finally justifies some of the commissioner’s prior executive speeches.

Li-Fo

Not the loudest week, just the richest one. The skyline remains his, whether anyone enjoys acknowledging that or not.

Providencemike

Less dead than before, which in this room absolutely counts as progress. A cleaner line and a little actual air.

The Commissioner’s Self-Esteem

Recovered sharply and is now expected to become briefly unbearable.

Red

The nastiest collapse at the table. One week you are profitable, the next you are explaining yourself to the spreadsheet like it is a parole board.

Hiro

Cooled from dangerous to disappointing in a hurry. More volume, worse money, and no argument strong enough to rescue the screenshot.

Nitro

Still the room’s official museum exhibit for low-profit optimism and structurally unsound confidence.

The Rest of the Room

Forced to watch the commish win a week and Li-Fo still win the larger argument. That is a rough civic burden.

BM

BostnMike finally posted the week he’s been describing since Christmas: more entries, more hits, more placements, and a profit jump big enough to turn the group chat into an eye-roll convention. Credit where it’s due — the man was excellent. He was also immediately unbearable.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • BostnMike: from $240 to $390, with hits rising from 19 to 23 and placements from 8 to 10 — the cleanest heater on the page.
  • Li-Fo: from $400 to $435 and cashes from 10 to 11, because apparently even other people’s good weeks still need his permission.
  • Providencemike: from -$135 to -$60, which is not joy exactly, but it is much closer to oxygen.
  • Red: from $225 to -$100, a swing so ugly it practically arrived with paperwork.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • The commish finally had a week worth printing and laminating.
  • Li-Fo remains the room’s financial landlord, now with even more rent history.
  • Providencemike improved enough to qualify as medically responsive.
  • Hiro reminded the room that heaters are rentals, not inheritance plans.
  • Nitro continues to underwrite the low-profit arts with unwavering personal commitment.
  • Red had the kind of week you try not to describe in front of witnesses.
  • Phattedcalf makes the New Blood roll call, which means the league once again welcomed a new face with chips, chaos, and a receipt.
  • No one has yet convinced the spreadsheet to become sentimental.
  • The room got one commissioner heater and still could not escape Li-Fo’s tax bracket.
  • By late February, the standings were less a competition than a zoning map with victims.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was February 27: the commish got hot, Li-Fo got richer, Providencemike found a little daylight, and Red discovered a trap door nobody asked for. It was one of those perfect TLPT weeks where a player could absolutely win the story without ever fully taking control of the building. The receipts, as always, remain public.

weeks.title: The Week That Was
weeks.dek: The top of the league stayed expensive, but underneath it, NASA Al surged, Hiro got sharper, Red kept climbing, and the commish found fresh ways to turn participation into paperwork.
weeks.html:
By February 21, the top of the room still looked familiar. Li-Fo kept the deed, the money stayed where it usually stays, and the penthouse remained closed to the general public. Underneath that, though, the middle of the table got noticeably sharper. NASA Al surged again, Hiro looked alive, Red kept stacking respectable results, and the room briefly resembled a functioning ecosystem instead of a collection of bad personal decisions.

“The top stayed rich. The middle got sharper.”

🎯 The Big Story

The cleanest week belonged to NASA Al, who did not just improve — he took control. Profit climbed from $290 to $330, hits rose from 12 to 14, placements moved from 6 to 7, and by the time the dust settled he had claimed Power Leader and Top True Skill. That is not just a nice week. That is a man filing flight plans while the rest of the room is still looking for parking.

What makes the week more interesting, though, is that he was not alone. Hiro sharpened up. Red kept climbing. Even the overall texture of the table looked healthier in the middle, which is unusual enough around here to count as a plot development. The top may not have changed ownership, but the tenants underneath it definitely got louder.

NA
Week’s Sharpest Rise
NASA Al
Profit ↑Hits ↑Placements ↑Power Leader ↑

At the very top, Li-Fo still looked like the room’s long-term real-estate problem. His weekly line did not need to be flashy because the larger board still answered to him. Profit stayed parked at $400, the money crown did not move, and the whole thing continued to read like a rich man’s refusal to be rushed.

And because no TLPT week is complete without one player volunteering as a cautionary memo, BostnMike once again handled the administrative burden personally. More entries, no gain in hits, no gain in placements, one more bubble, and less money. The middle got sharper. The commissioner got more ceremonial.

📈 Movers

NASA Al

The best week on the page. Cleaner, stronger, richer, and now sitting on Power Leader with the kind of line that makes everybody else sound defensive.

Hiro

A strong rebound lane. More profit, more hits, another placement, and the kind of useful grind that quietly becomes a problem for everyone else.

Red

Still doing the annoying thing where the profile looks slightly unhinged and the outcome still lands on the respectable side of the TLPT.

The Middle Class

For one week, at least, the room underneath the penthouse actually looked alive.

BostnMike

Another entry, another bubble, same hit count, same placement count, less profit. A week of pure commissioner paperwork.

Nitro

The floor somehow found a basement. The low-profit arts remain fully endowed.

Jeff T

More effort, slightly less catastrophe, but still nowhere near what most people would describe as encouraging.

The Bubble Economy

Still thriving, thanks largely to one extremely committed commissioner.

BM

BostnMike treated the week like a ribbon-cutting ceremony for disappointment: showed up for everything, improved nothing, added a bubble, and left with less money. Some men collect results. The commish collects irony.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • NASA Al: from $290 to $330, with hits climbing from 12 to 14 and placements from 6 to 7 — the cleanest heater at the table.
  • Hiro: from $45 to $105, with more hits and another placement, which is exactly how grinders make themselves everybody else’s problem.
  • Red: from $185 to $225, continuing the deeply annoying tradition of making chaos look productive.
  • BostnMike: from $270 to $240 while bubbles climbed from 5 to 6, which is less a slump than a government-issued identity.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • NASA Al did not just have the week. He took the controls.
  • Li-Fo remained rich enough that everyone else’s plans still felt like leasing.
  • Hiro looked sharper, which is bad news for anyone hoping the room would get easier.
  • Red continues to operate like a statistical dare that occasionally cashes.
  • The middle of the pack finally looked competent enough to cause discomfort.
  • BostnMike somehow turned visibility into a tax bracket nobody wanted.
  • Nitro remains the room’s most committed investor in low-return art.
  • Jeff T is still out here negotiating with gravity and mostly losing.
  • No new blood this week — just the same cast refining old mistakes.
  • The top of the room stayed expensive. The middle finally started charging rent.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was February 21: the top of the league stayed rich, the middle got sharper, NASA Al grabbed the loudest rise, Hiro and Red added real support, and BostnMike once again proved that activity and reward remain only loosely affiliated. The hierarchy held. The pressure underneath it finally got interesting.

weeks.title: The Week That Was
weeks.dek: NASA Al launched, Li-Fo still owned the sky, and the rest of the room spent another week learning that effort and reward remain only loosely acquainted.
weeks.html:
By February 13, the room finally gave someone else the cleanest weekly headline. NASA Al took off. The profit line jumped, the hits piled up, the placements improved, and for one very tidy stretch, the table looked like it had been handed over to a man operating with a checklist instead of a prayer. The larger skyline, though, still belonged to Li-Fo, who kept the financial penthouse even while someone else won the week.

“NASA Al launched. Li-Fo still owned the sky.”

🎯 The Big Story

The best week on the night belonged to NASA Al. Profit jumped from $115 to $290, hits climbed from 8 to 12, placements rose from 4 to 6, and the whole thing read like the sort of heater the rest of the room normally insists only happens to other people. It was clean, forceful, and annoyingly efficient.

That still did not make him the owner of the skyline. Li-Fo kept that title. Even with a small dip in profit, he remained Profit Leader, still sitting on the room’s biggest money line and still carrying the kind of record-page presence that makes everyone else sound like a tenant when they talk about their long-term plans.

NA
Week’s Cleanest Launch
NASA Al
Profit ↑Hits ↑Placements ↑Mission Cleared

Below that top story, the middle of the room actually got healthier for a change. Ahmed improved, Red improved, and the supporting cast finally looked less like collateral damage and more like active participants. Naturally, that also meant somebody had to volunteer as the cautionary example. The commissioner was kind enough to handle it himself.

📈 Movers

NASA Al

The week’s best heater, full stop. Bigger profit, more hits, more placements, and the cleanest line on the page.

Ahmed

Quietly strong. More hits, another placement, and the kind of week that sneaks up on the room after everyone else has already been too loud.

Red

Still doing the statistically annoying thing where the aggression looks suspicious and the outcome somehow stays respectable.

Li-Fo

Not the best week, but still the league’s permanent address. The skyline remains his until someone proves otherwise.

BostnMike

One more entry, one more hit, thirty fewer dollars, and no extra placement to justify any of it. A week of administrative effort and financial insult.

Hiro

More entries, same hits, same placements, less profit. The grind remained. The reward trimmed itself.

Nitro

Still a permanent monument to low-profit optimism and a deck that appears to have filed a complaint.

Jeff T

At this point the numbers are not being rude anymore. They are just being consistent.

BM

BostnMike put together the kind of week only a commissioner could love: enough activity to call it productive, enough slippage to call it annoying, and just enough statistical evidence to lose the argument at home anyway.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • NASA Al: from $115 to $290, with hits rising from 8 to 12 and placements from 4 to 6 — the week’s cleanest lift.
  • Li-Fo: still Profit Leader and still the room’s long-term real estate magnate, even on a week where somebody else had the cleaner surge.
  • Ahmed: from $30 to $80 with more hits and another placement, which is exactly the kind of rise that gets overlooked until it matters.
  • BostnMike: from $300 to $270 despite more volume, which is a very specific and very commissioner-shaped form of suffering.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • NASA Al did not just improve. He filed a flight plan.
  • Li-Fo lost a little altitude and still remained everybody’s landlord.
  • Ahmed had one of the week’s best quiet rises, which is usually the sort that ages well.
  • Red continues to make “that shouldn’t work” look statistically viable.
  • Hiro stayed productive enough to matter and expensive enough to annoy himself.
  • Yann arrived and immediately started collecting crowns like the room had been saving them for him.
  • Fabian also made his first appearance, which means the league once again welcomed new blood with paperwork and pain.
  • Nitro remains a publicly funded cautionary tale.
  • Jeff T is still available for further statistical hardship, apparently by appointment.
  • The room got better this week. The commissioner mostly got busier.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was February 13: NASA Al had the launch, Li-Fo kept the skyline, the middle of the room finally showed signs of competent life, and BostnMike once again proved that increased participation is not a legally binding path to increased joy. The league moved. The hierarchy mostly didn’t. The spreadsheet, as always, kept the receipts.

weeks.title: The Week That Was
weeks.dek: Li-Fo widened the gap, Hiro kept grinding, and BostnMike somehow managed to lose money while making the Bubble King belt even heavier.
weeks.html:
By February 7, the room was starting to look less like a league and more like a zoning dispute. Li-Fo kept expanding the top-floor footprint, Hiro kept quietly adding useful work, and BostnMike once again found the rare statistical lane where increased involvement somehow produced less money and more embarrassment. In other words, normal service resumed.

“Li-Fo widened the gap. BostnMike kept the curse.”

🎯 The Big Story

The cleanest big-picture story of the week belonged to Li-Fo. Profit jumped from $360 to $430, cashes climbed from 9 to 10, and the entire top end of the table looked a little more distant for everyone else. This was not one flashy night. This was a richer man becoming harder to catch.

The subtle counterpoint belonged to Hiro, who did not post the biggest money week but kept stacking the sort of numbers grinders secretly admire. weeks.html:
By the last snapshot of January, the room finally gave BostnMike something he could point to besides administrative burden. The commish had himself a week. Profit jumped, hits climbed, placements improved, and for once the spreadsheet looked less like a disciplinary memo and more like a receipt worth framing. Of course, because this is TLPT, even a good week could not fully disinfect the accessories.

“The commish finally got paid. The belt, tragically, remained.”

🎯 The Big Story

The cleanest headline of the week belonged to BostnMike. He went from $240 to $330 in profit, added another entry, picked up three more hits, and improved from 7 to 8 placements. In a room where activity often disguises itself as progress, this was one of the rarer weeks where the activity actually cashed the check.

That still did not mean he owned the building. Li-Fo kept that job. The profit crown stayed put at $360, the top-floor view remained unchanged, and the broader money conversation still ran through him. So yes, the commish had the week. Li-Fo still had the property line.

BM

BostnMike
Profit ↑Hits ↑Placements ↑Still Bubble King

If the commish owned the main event, NASA Al had the cleanest supporting launch. His profit jumped from $115 to $255, his hit count spiked from 8 to 11, and he quietly put together the sort of week that makes everyone else wonder whether they missed a meeting. It was the kind of rise that does not scream for attention, which usually means it deserves even more of it.

Elsewhere, January continued doing what January does best: giving a few players false hope and billing the rest accordingly. Hiro cooled off after a stronger run, Cougar gave back a little ground, and A.I. Dave once again demonstrated that more volume is not legally required to produce a better outcome.

📈 Movers

BostnMike

The week’s biggest winner and, against all odds, an actual success story. More profit, more hits, more placements, and fewer immediate complaints from the bankroll.

NASA Al

Li-Fo

Did not need the flashiest week because he is still the man everyone sees when they check the money board.

The Commissioner’s Ego

Recovered nicely after several weeks of fully justified public skepticism.

Hiro

The strongest cool-off lane. More entries, no improvement in hits or cashes, and a profit line that remembered gravity.

Cougar

Still dangerous, still relevant, but the week looked more like a comedown than a sequel.

A.I. Dave

Another week where activity and reward chose not to travel together.

The Rest of the Room

Still trying to prove that January is a new beginning when the evidence keeps suggesting it is just another tab.

BM

Well, well, well — the commish finally had a week that matched the speeches. The profit was real, the hits were real, the placements were real, and for one brief moment even the spreadsheet had to admit he wasn’t just filibustering with chips.

  • BostnMike: from $240 to $330, with hits climbing from 15 to 18 and placements from 7 to 8.

  • No new blood, just the same cast finding fresher ways to disappoint themselves.
  • The spreadsheet continues to provide all the closure no one asked for.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was January 31: the commish finally got paid, NASA Al launched right behind him, Li-Fo kept the penthouse, and the rest of the room was left to explain why a new month still looked suspiciously like the old one. A good week can change the mood. Around here, it rarely changes the hierarchy.

weeks.html:
By January 24, the room had settled into a familiar rhythm: the top of the table stayed stubbornly intact, but underneath it, a few souls were either recovering, unraveling, or pretending those were the same thing. The cleanest sign of life came from Wild Bill, who finally gave the spreadsheet something to work with besides concern. Li-Fo, meanwhile, remained exactly what he had already been — the man with the keys.

“Wild Bill found a pulse. Li-Fo kept the building.”

🎯 The Big Story

The week’s best story belonged to Wild Bill. Not because he suddenly took over the room, and not because anyone is carving his face into the honors page just yet, but because he finally looked alive. Profit improved from -$150 to -$60, hits doubled from 2 to 4, and he notched his first cash. In TLPT terms, that counts as both progress and a public statement.

At the top, though, the landlord situation remained unchanged. Li-Fo still sat on $360 profit and kept the broader room-wide claim. That is the trick of this league: somebody can have the week, but somebody else still owns the neighborhood. Wild Bill found daylight. Li-Fo kept the deed.

WB
Week’s Best Recovery
Wild Bill
Profit ↑Hits ↑First CashActually Breathing

That does not mean the room suddenly got healthier. Providencemike dug deeper, Jeff T kept finding new ways to worsen an already ugly month, and The Architect slipped just enough to make the week feel like a quiet structural defect. Even BostnMike, who actually had a decent week, could not escape the indignity of the accessory shelf.

📈 Movers

Wild Bill

The cleanest heater of the week, or at least the closest thing to one. He found profit, found hits, and finally found a cash.

BostnMike

A genuinely solid week by commissioner standards: more profit, more hits, more placements, and still somehow not enough dignity to escape the cursed jewelry.

Li-Fo

Not the flashiest weekly move, but still the man everybody sees when they look at the money board.

The Concept of Hope

Wild Bill at least kept it alive for another week.

Providencemike

The hole got deeper, the luck profile got uglier, and the spreadsheet continued to read like a warning label.

Jeff T

Another week, another step in the wrong direction. At some point this stops being variance and starts feeling like a recurring charge.

The Architect

The blueprint may still be elegant, but the weekly numbers looked a little less structurally sound this time around.

The Room

Still mistaking persistence for progress, which is one of the league’s most cherished traditions.

BM

The commish finally turned in a week that looked competent on purpose. Naturally, he celebrated by remaining just self-aware enough to know nobody was throwing him a parade for it.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • Wild Bill: from -$150 to -$60, with hits doubling and the first cash finally arriving.
  • Li-Fo: still sitting on $360 profit and still occupying the room’s financial penthouse.
  • BostnMike: from $165 to $240, which qualifies as a real week even if the crown shelf remains deeply unserious.
  • Providencemike: from -$240 to -$270, proving once again that the bottom can, in fact, subcontract.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • Wild Bill finally gave the spreadsheet a reason to unclench.
  • Li-Fo continues to run the kind of profit profile that makes everyone else sound defensive.
  • BostnMike had a good week and still somehow looked roastable. That is talent.
  • Jeff T is building a résumé for suffering nobody asked to review.
  • The Architect’s latest numbers suggest the permits may still be pending.
  • Providencemike remains the room’s leading advocate for negative case studies.
  • Hayden D makes an early appearance and gets the standard TLPT welcome package: a seat, a sweat, and a bill.
  • No one has yet convinced January to become kinder than December.
  • The spreadsheet still has no sympathy setting.
  • At the top, nothing changed. Underneath, the noise stayed fully operational.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was January 24: Wild Bill finally found a pulse, Li-Fo never stopped owning the property, and the rest of the room continued its civic duty of generating cautionary examples. The top of the league table stayed still. The mess underneath remained beautifully active. The spreadsheet, naturally, wrote it all down.

The loudest weekly move belonged to Cougar, who put together the cleanest jump on the leaderboard. The bigger room-wide truth, though, was that Li-Fo still looked like the man holding the deed.

“Cougar stole the week. Li-Fo kept the building.”

🎯 The Big Story

The sharpest weekly surge belonged to Cougar. He went from $30 to $180 in profit, added hits, added another cash, and generally spent the week behaving like someone who had stopped mistaking chaos for a business plan. It was not just a nice little bump. It was the cleanest, clearest heater at the table.

That still did not make him the room’s landlord. Li-Fo remained that. While Cougar owned the week, Li-Fo kept the broader power position, adding profit and another cash while continuing to sit on top of the money conversation. At some point it stops being a hot run and starts looking like property ownership.

Of course, this room never gives you one clean story without dragging three dirtier ones behind it. Jeff T had the week’s best collapse, Hiro cooled off after the stronger January opening, and BostnMike once again found a way to increase his workload without improving his mood. The week’s clearest heater. More profit, more hits, another cash, and an actual argument for competence.

weeks.html:
The first game of the new year answered the only question that really mattered: would the room come back refreshed, disciplined, and ready to make better decisions? Of course not. What it did produce, however, was a very clean early-year headline. Hiro opened 2026 with the strongest jump on the league table, Li-Fo stayed rich enough to remain everyone’s financial point of reference, and the rest of the room got right back to its usual mix of optimism and self-harm.

“The calendar changed. The suffering kept its seat.”

🎯 The Big Story

The cleanest story of the week was Hiro. He started the year at $40 in profit and ended this snapshot at $190, while adding hits and another cash along the way. More importantly, he walked out of the first game of 2026 as the new Hit King, which is the sort of title that sounds ceremonial until you realize it means he was quietly cashing while everybody else was explaining themselves.

That does not mean Li-Fo disappeared. Far from it. Li-Fo still sat atop the profit board, still owned the money conversation, and still looked like the player most likely to demand rent from the honors page. But this week, the spotlight shifted. Li-Fo stayed rich. Hiro stole the headline.

HI
New Year’s Strongest Surge
Hiro
Profit ↑Hits ↑Cashes ↑Hit King ↑

Elsewhere, at the table looked familiar in the most TLPT way possible. Li-Fo stayed rich. BostnMike stayed busy. Chris O cooled off. And the room, in a very reassuring display of consistency, needed absolutely no warm-up period to start punishing itself again.

📈 Movers

Hiro

The week’s clearest riser and the best opening act of 2026. Profit jumped, hits climbed, and the Hit King belt changed hands.

Li-Fo

Still the profit boss. Not the loudest weekly jump, but league still runs through him whether anyone enjoys that fact or not.

Red

Still lingering in the positive and continuing to produce the kind of profile that suggests chaos can occasionally stumble into results.

Big Tim

Did not suddenly become the center of the room, but continued to sit in that annoyingly respectable zone where things mostly make sense.

Chris O

The December shine faded a bit. Still relevant, still in the mix, but no longer the cleanest feel-good line on the stat hub.

BostnMike

More involvement, less reward. The sort of week that makes a player feel active right up until the bankroll starts asking harder questions.

Nitro

The new year did not magically improve the fundamentals. The low-profit arts remain fully funded.

The Whole Room

No true newcomer story, no reset button, just the same league picking up exactly where it left off — with confidence outpacing evidence.

BM

BostnMike opened the year like a man trying to win through administrative persistence: lots of activity, plenty of fingerprints, and a final result that still suggested the universe had read none of his memos.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • Hiro: from $40 to $190, with more hits and another cash — the cleanest week-over-week surge on the leaderboard.
  • Li-Fo: still Profit Leader, which means even on a week where the spotlight shifted, the money conversation still began with him.
  • BostnMike: more entries and more hits, but profit slipped from $225 to $195 — an efficient summary of commissioner life.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • Li-Fo remained the room’s financial landlord, even if this week’s spotlight moved elsewhere.

  • Big Tim continued to occupy the deeply annoying territory of quiet competence.

  • The room needed exactly zero adjustment period to resume making terrible emotional investments.
  • Red continues to feel like a man trying to win arguments and pots with equal aggression.
  • No new blood, just the same cast returning to the scene of the crime.
  • 2026 may be new. The spreadsheet’s sense of humor is not.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was the first game of 2026: Hiro grabbed the week, Li-Fo kept the money crown, and the rest of the league wasted no time proving that a new calendar does not automatically produce better judgment. The year changed. The hierarchy only adjusted slightly. The chaos, reassuringly, remained fully operational.

weeks.title: The Week That Was
weeks.dek: The whole league got rewritten: Li-Fo seized the top shelf, BostnMike tumbled into bubble duty, and a couple of the season’s longest-running pain stories suddenly found daylight.
weeks.html:
By December 27, the season stopped looking settled and started looking edited. The names were familiar, but the hierarchy wasn’t. What had felt like a long, slow hardening of roles suddenly broke apart in one violent little holiday rewrite. The top changed hands. The basement changed addresses. Even the side plots got ambitious.

“The whole league got rewritten.”

🎯 The Big Story

The cleanest story was Li-Fo finally turning all that weird suspended energy into something unmistakable. For weeks he had been hovering in the strangest possible state — technically alive, emotionally suspicious, permanently bubbling around the edge of relevance. Then all at once the whole thing resolved. Profit shot from +$5 to +$370, the hits piled up, the placements arrived, and the honors page suddenly looked like it had been repossessed in his name. By the end of the snapshot he was Profit Leader, Power Leader, Clutch Leader, and Hit King, along with the rest of the serious hardware.

That alone would have been enough for a proper season-ending column. But what made the week feel bigger was the collateral. BostnMike didn’t just lose a little ground. He dropped from the league’s operating system to Bubble King, still visible but now wearing a very different kind of crown. Jeff T and Wild Bill, two men who had spent months running a joint experiment in statistical suffering, somehow found oxygen at the same time. The whole table tilted.

LF
Week’s Full Rewrite
Li-Fo
Profit LeaderPower LeaderClutch LeaderHit King

What makes this snapshot so satisfying is that it doesn’t behave like a tidy finale. It behaves like a room full of unfinished business breaking loose at once. Hiro stayed relevant but dimmer. BostnMike lost altitude in public. Jeff T and Wild Bill clawed their way back into the light. And Li-Fo, of all people, finally stopped hovering and picked a side.

📈 Movers

Li-Fo

From long-running side mystery to full-blown ruler. The weird little purgatory arc finally paid off in cash and hardware.

Jeff T

One of the wildest reversals in the whole archive run. From deep-red cautionary tale to technically positive, which almost feels impolite after everything.

Wild Bill

Finally found daylight. Not a coronation, but enough life to make the season’s earlier suffering look slightly less terminal.

The League’s Sense Of Drama

Closed the year by overachieving.

BostnMike

Months of municipal control, and then a holiday season reminder that empires do not always get a graceful year-end review.

Hiro

Still relevant, still useful, but clearly no longer the center of the table’s imagination.

ProvidenceMike

Now buried far enough down to inherit the uglier side of the basement story.

Stability

Completely abandoned by the final snapshot of the year.

BM

BostnMike spent months turning the league into his personal municipality, then closed the year by discovering that empires can, in fact, misplace the keys. A strong run, a weak landing, and suddenly the bubble paperwork looked a lot more personal.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • Li-Fo: from +$5 to +$370, with hits jumping from 10 to 18 and placements from 2 to 8 — the cleanest total rewrite on the page.
  • BostnMike: from +$825 down to +$225, a swing large enough to turn the season’s ruler into the season’s newest warning label.
  • Jeff T: from -$510 to +$25, a recovery so sharp it feels like someone corrected the spreadsheet by hand.
  • Wild Bill: from -$480 to +$5, which may not be glory, but after the year he had, it absolutely counts as oxygen.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • Li-Fo finally answered the question by taking the whole room with him.
  • BostnMike discovered that long stretches of competence do not automatically come with a warranty.
  • Jeff T chose late December to become a functioning participant, which is one way to keep a season memorable.
  • Wild Bill finally gave the numbers a reason to soften their tone.
  • Hiro remained in the picture, just no longer holding the camera.
  • No new blood spotlight this week — the existing cast did more than enough damage on its own.
  • The year did not end with confirmation. It ended with revision.
  • Some seasons settle down before the finale. This one kicked the door open instead.
  • The league spent months building a hierarchy and one week taking a sledgehammer to it.
  • By the end of December, TLPT was no longer revealing habits. It was revealing volatility.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was December 27: the league got rewritten, Li-Fo seized the crown, BostnMike fell out of office, and a couple of the season’s longest-running pain stories finally found daylight at the exact moment nobody expected them to. It was messy, abrupt, and exactly the kind of ending a league like this deserves.

weeks.title: The Week That Was
weeks.dek: Hiro outlasted the noise, the leader kept making the top feel closed for business, and the rest of the league kept revealing who was built for a long winter and who was just built for excuses.
weeks.html:
By December 21, the season had stopped pretending every week needed a fresh star. Some players had become weather. Some had become caution signs. And some, like Hiro, had become something a little harder to dismiss: durable. Not flashy enough to own the room, not messy enough to become a punchline, just stubbornly present every time the standings tried to move on without him.

“Hiro outlasted the noise.”

🎯 The Big Story

The cleanest story this week belonged to Hiro, and it belonged to him for the least glamorous reason possible: he was still there. Another entry, another hit, same $85 profit line, and another week where his season kept refusing to turn into either a collapse or a coronation. In a league that spends half its time overreacting to spikes and the other half falling through trap doors, that kind of persistence starts to look like its own form of authority.

What makes Hiro useful as the center of this week is that he gives the season texture. BostnMike is still the giant fact sitting over everything, extending the lead again and making the upper tier feel like private property. Li-Fo is still wandering around with that eerie little +$5 life-support profile and three bubbles hanging off it. But Hiro is the guy who keeps making all of that feel grounded. He is not the loudest player in the league. He is the one still visible after the shouting stops.

HI
Week’s Most Durable Presence
Hiro
Hits ↑Entries ↑Profit FlatStill In Frame

The rest of the week only made that contrast clearer. BostnMike kept widening the gap without needing to raise his voice. Li-Fo stayed in his strange little half-life. And down below, Jeff T and Wild Bill continued their long-running argument with gravity, each one adding more evidence that some seasons do not break bad in one moment — they just keep getting heavier.

📈 Movers

Hiro

Still relevant, still useful, still refusing to leave the serious part of the conversation.

BostnMike

Another extension of the lead, another reminder that the top of the league now feels less like a race and more like an access issue.

A.I. Dave

Still neat, still positive, still making competence feel vaguely smug without ever becoming noisy about it.

The Players Who Survived December

At this point, staying coherent counts as a category.

Li-Fo

Still technically alive, still emotionally strange, still one of the hardest profiles in the league to describe without sounding worried.

Jeff T

Another step lower, still no hits, still no cashes, and increasingly the kind of line that should come with a support hotline.

Wild Bill

The year keeps pitching him dramatic lighting and no payoff.

The Floor

Still moving, and unfortunately not upward.

BM

BostnMike has now been good for so long that the table doesn’t even argue with it anymore. It just updates the numbers and sighs, which is about as close to respect as a frontrunner should ever expect around here.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • Hiro: still at +$85, now over 16 entries with 13 hits — not dominant, just impossible to erase.
  • BostnMike: up to $825 profit, 28 hits, and 13 cashes, which by now reads less like a season and more like a tax bracket.
  • Li-Fo: still parked at +$5 with 3 bubbles, somehow managing to feel both present and ghostly at the same time.
  • Jeff T: down to -$510 with 0 hits and 0 cashes, still the cleanest long-term proof that participation is not a strategy.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • Hiro has become the league’s answer to background radiation: never the whole story, always part of the environment.
  • BostnMike keeps extending the top without ever needing a dramatic finish.
  • A.I. Dave remains one of the table’s quieter annoyances, which is usually the most dangerous kind.
  • Li-Fo has now turned five dollars into a multi-week existential condition.
  • The middle of the league did not crack. It congealed.
  • Jeff T is now so deep in the red he should start getting seasonal mail there.
  • Wild Bill is still waiting for the version of this story where endurance gets rewarded instead of itemized.
  • No new blood spotlight this week — just familiar names becoming harder to misread.
  • The season is no longer interested in surprises. It prefers confirmation.
  • By late December, the table wasn’t changing identities anymore. It was revealing durability.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was December 21: Hiro outlasted the noise, the leader kept widening the horizon, and the rest of the league kept proving that consistency can be every bit as revealing as chaos. By then, TLPT wasn’t asking who might emerge. It was showing who was still standing.

weeks.dek: The league started looking more settled at the top, but underneath it Jeff T and Wild Bill kept digging, Li-Fo got stranger, and the middle of the table stayed just crowded enough to stay interesting.
weeks.html:
By August 23, TLPT had moved out of its early-chaos phase and into something more dangerous: structure. The surprises were fewer. The hierarchy was clearer. The league was starting to look less like a restart and more like a place where people were settling into jobs, habits, and reputations they might not be able to shake for a while.

“The league settled. The pain didn’t.”

🎯 The Big Story

The biggest development this week was not a dramatic overthrow. It was stability. BostnMike stayed on top, added more profit, more hits, and another cash, and continued to make the upper tier of the league feel increasingly official. This was no longer a hot run or a funny little commissioner subplot. At this point it was starting to feel like policy.

That did not mean the rest of the table went quiet. It just got more specific. Hiro remained stubbornly relevant, the kind of player who never quite leaves the frame. A.I. Dave stayed efficient enough to be irritating. Li-Fo drifted into one of the strangest profiles in the league — still positive, technically still viable, but somehow looking less convincing while remaining hard to dismiss. And at the bottom, the usual civic services remained fully operational.

BM
Week’s Most Established Problem
BostnMike
Profit ↑Hits ↑Cashes ↑Still Running Things

The more interesting wrinkle, honestly, was how much clearer the supporting cast had become. Hiro was no longer the opening-night monarch, but he was still hanging around with enough volume and enough results to remain annoying. Li-Fo looked less like a pursuer and more like a man wandering around the edge of contention with unresolved bubble issues. And Jeff T and Wild Bill continued their long-running social experiment into how much suffering a poker league can document before somebody starts charging admission.

📈 Movers

BostnMike

Another strong week, another firm grip on the league’s top shelf, and another reminder that this is starting to feel annoyingly legitimate.

Hiro

Still not gone, still productive, still lurking around the serious part of the conversation like he never really accepted the rewrite.

A.I. Dave

Efficient, tidy, and increasingly the sort of player people mention quietly because they do not want to encourage him.

The Middle of the Table

Not glamorous, but finally coherent enough to feel like it matters.

Li-Fo

Still above water, but only barely, and now carrying enough bubble energy to make every positive line feel conditional.

Jeff T

Another week deeper, another week hitless, and another week that read like the league was billing him monthly.

Wild Bill

The comeback still has atmosphere, but somebody misplaced the revenue model.

Hope

Still technically present, just increasingly underqualified for the room.

BM

BostnMike has now been on top long enough that the league can’t call it a run anymore. It has become policy, which is much worse for morale and somehow even worse for his humility.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • BostnMike: up to $330 profit, 12 hits, and 5 cashes — no longer a heater, just the current operating system.
  • Hiro: still at $85, now over 6 entries with 5 hits, which makes him less explosive and more stubbornly relevant.
  • Li-Fo: still sitting at $5 profit, which is technically positive and spiritually hilarious.
  • Jeff T: down to -$240 with 0 hits and 0 cashes, a line so cleanly miserable it barely needs adjectives.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • The league is no longer improvising. It is developing habits.
  • BostnMike now feels less like a surprise and more like an administrative burden everyone has to plan around.
  • Hiro remains too present to ignore and too stable to dismiss.
  • A.I. Dave continues to look like the answer key in a room full of guesses.
  • Li-Fo has somehow turned five dollars into a full personality arc.
  • Jeff T is still underwriting the cautionary side of the table.
  • Wild Bill remains committed to the dramatic version of patience.
  • No new blood spotlight this week — just the same cast settling deeper into character.
  • The league feels more real now, which is not the same as kinder.
  • By late August, TLPT had stopped introducing itself and started revealing what kind of place it really was.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was August 23: the league got more settled, the middle of the table got more believable, and the basement kept charging rent like nothing had changed. The names were familiar by then. The roles were becoming permanent. And the league, at last, was starting to look like itself.

weeks.html:
By July 26, the league finally looked less like a string of isolated outbursts and more like an actual ecosystem. The top was no longer changing hands every five minutes. The middle had stopped pretending it was harmless. And the bottom, in a show of admirable consistency, remained fully committed to the arts of pain, denial, and rebuy-adjacent despair.

“The table got less dramatic and more real.”

🎯 The Big Story

The biggest development this week was not a fresh shock. It was structure. BostnMike still had the whole top shelf: Profit Leader, Power Leader, Clutch Leader, Hit King, and the matching record line to make the whole thing feel increasingly official. The numbers were no longer flirting with the idea that he might be hot. They were beginning to accept that this was just the local government now.

That did not make the rest of the room uninteresting. It made it more specific. Li-Fo stopped looking like the main challenger and started looking like the strangest subplot on the page — still positive, still relevant, but now carrying the bubble story like a man who refuses to go broke and refuses to arrive cleanly. Hiro stayed stubbornly in the picture, not flashy enough to dominate but still annoying enough to matter. And the old pain lanes, bless them, stayed faithfully open.

BM
Week’s Most Stable Problem
BostnMike
Profit LeaderPower LeaderClutch LeaderHit King

The funniest part of the week may have been that the supporting cast actually got more textured. A.I. Dave remained annoyingly efficient. Rob F continued to look like the kind of low-volume side quest that steals a trophy when you stop paying attention. Li-Fo’s line became less “threatening landlord” and more “successful man with unresolved bubble issues.” And Jeff T, unfortunately, kept operating as a very persuasive anti-gambling pamphlet.

📈 Movers

BostnMike

No longer a streak, no longer a surprise, now just the face of the league and it's most permanent inconvenience.

Hiro

Still hanging around the fight, still useful, still the most durable reminder that opening-night talent does not disappear just because someone else took the throne.

A.I. Dave

Quietly efficient, still positive, and increasingly the kind of name that belongs in side conversations nobody wants the rest of the room hearing.

Rob F

Small sample, loud nuisance. Still one of the cleanest tiny-volume irritants on at the table.

Li-Fo

Still solvent, still present, but somehow now carrying more bubble energy than menace.

Jeff T

The basement remains fully furnished and, apparently, non-refundable.

Wild Bill

The comeback tour still has atmosphere, but very little box office.

The Illusion of Easy Reads

Gone. This board now comes with subplots.

BM

BostnMike has reached the worst possible stage of a heater: the part where even his enemies have to admit it’s starting to look sustainable. That is great news for him, terrible news for the room, and morally offensive to anyone who preferred him as a lovable administrative error.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • BostnMike: up to $270 profit, 10 hits, 4 cashes, and now holding the entire top shelf like a man who has stopped asking permission.
  • Li-Fo: down from $35 to $5, now with 2 bubbles — still alive, just increasingly weird about it.
  • Hiro: still at $85, now over 5 entries, which makes him less a flare-up and more a permanent background concern.
  • Jeff T: down to -$210 and still sitting on Lowest Profit / Worst Luck, which is less a run than a zip code.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • BostnMike is now less a story and more a standing civic issue.
  • Li-Fo somehow turned “still positive” into “mildly haunted.”
  • Hiro remains annoyingly hard to evict from relevance.
  • A.I. Dave continues to look like he solves poker in a spreadsheet before dinner.
  • Rob F is still the statistical version of a guy who shows up late and somehow leaves with your watch.
  • Jeff T is making a compelling case for adding handrails to the basement.
  • Wild Bill is still waiting for the profitable cut of this season.
  • No new blood spotlight this week — just the existing cast getting stranger and more settled at the same time.
  • The table is not simpler now. It is just more honest.
  • By late July, TLPT was no longer introducing itself. It was revealing habits.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was July 26: the top of the league table stopped feeling temporary, the middle found its own weird little ambitions, and the basement continued serving as the league’s most dependable public utility. The league was fully in motion now. Not cleaner. Not kinder. Just more real.

weeks.title: The Week That Was

weeks.html:
By June 28, the question was no longer whether BostnMike had stolen a week. The question was whether the room had a real commissioner problem on its hands. One heater is a plot twist. Two in a row starts to feel like policy. And while the rest of the table was still trying to decide whether this was sustainable, Li-Fo quietly started building the only serious chase on for the league crown.

“The commish built a sequel. Li-Fo built a chase.”

🎯 The Big Story

BostnMike did not cool off. He escalated. Profit climbed from $180 to $320, hits jumped from 6 to 10, placements rose from 2 to 3, and the top shelf still belonged entirely to him. Profit Leader. Power Leader. Clutch Leader. Hit King. At this point the page no longer looks like a hot stretch. It looks like someone forgot to reinstall checks and balances after the hiatus.

The wrinkle that made the week more interesting was Li-Fo. While BostnMike kept the throne warm, Li-Fo made the strongest push underneath him, turning a modest positive line into a real pursuit. More profit, more hits, another cash, and suddenly the early board had an actual second act instead of just one man speaking from a balcony.

BM
Week’s Sequel Nobody Wanted
BostnMike
Profit ↑Hits ↑Placements ↑Still Owns The Shelf

What this really did was sharpen the room. Hiro was still around, still relevant, but no longer dictating the script. A.I. Dave and Rob F remained annoying little reminders that early new blood can linger. And Jeff T, in a much less glamorous subplot, continued proving that not every long-term story is aspirational.

📈 Movers

BostnMike

Not a fluke anymore. Another surge, another week on top, and the kind of follow-up that makes even his critics start sounding tired.

Li-Fo

The clearest chase story in the league. Sharper line, stronger cash profile, and the first real hint that the top might not stay uncontested forever.

Hiro

No longer the king, but still standing close enough to the throne to make everyone uncomfortable.

The Standings Tension

For the first time, this actually looked like a race and not just a lecture.

Jeff T

Still living in the basement with no hits, no cashes, and no visible appeal process.

Wild Bill

Another week in the red, another reminder that comeback stories are not automatically issued with every buy-in.

The Rest of The Room

Now forced to process the idea that the commissioner may have become a recurring problem instead of a weekly inconvenience.

Easy Narratives

Gone. The league table is starting to develop layers and bad intentions.

BM

BostnMike has now won often enough that the room may have to stop calling it a heater and start calling it a public relations problem. The numbers are no longer politely humoring him. They’re beginning to endorse the campaign.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • BostnMike: from $180 to $320, with hits rising from 6 to 10 and placements from 2 to 3 — the loudest follow-up of the week.
  • Li-Fo: from $35 to $105, with more hits, another placement, and a much more serious look at the top tier.
  • Jeff T: still buried at -$150 with no hits and no cashes, which is no longer a cold stretch so much as a postal address.
  • Hiro: still positive and still relevant, but now clearly part of the chase instead of the monarchy.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • The commish did not cool off. He franchised the bit.
  • Li-Fo finally gave the top of the table a pulse behind the leader.
  • Hiro is still in the story, just no longer writing it alone.
  • A.I. Dave and Rob F remain annoyingly real parts of the landscape.
  • Jeff T continues to perform long-term research into the limits of patience.
  • Wild Bill is still waiting for the profitable edit of this season.
  • No new blood this week — just the same names getting more complicated.
  • The room’s early hierarchy is no longer temporary enough to ignore.
  • The spreadsheet has already developed favorites and grudges.
  • By late June, this thing stopped feeling like a restart and started feeling like a regime.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was June 28: the commish built a sequel, Li-Fo built a chase, and the room finally got the kind of early-season tension that makes future weeks worth fearing in advance. A one-week wonder is easy to dismiss. A second straight takeover is how myths get drafted.

weeks.title: The Week That Was
weeks.dek: Opening-night Hiro is officially gone, BostnMike took the whole top shelf, and the league’s early hierarchy already looks like it was rewritten in permanent marker.
weeks.html:
Three snapshots into the relaunch, the early-season story has changed completely. Opening night belonged to Hiro. Episode two belonged to chaos. By June 21, the room had a new problem: the commish was no longer just talking like he ran the table — he actually did.

“Opening-night Hiro is officially gone. This is BostnMike’s mess now.”

🎯 The Big Story

The cleanest story of the week was not just that BostnMike stayed hot. It was that he turned one good follow-up into full control. Profit climbed from $105 to $180, hits rose from 4 to 6, placements moved from 1 to 2, and by the time the page settled he was sitting on Profit Leader, Power Leader, Clutch Leader, and Hit King. That is not a heater anymore. That is annexation with statistics.

The real point, though, is what it means for the tiny early history of the league. Hiro had owned the opener. On June 14, the table splintered. By June 21, the splinters were gone and BostnMike had stacked the whole top shelf in his own name. The relaunch stopped being a fun little shuffle and started becoming a power grab.

BM
Week’s Full Takeover
BostnMike
Profit LeaderPower LeaderClutch LeaderHit King

That did not mean the rest of the room vanished. Li-Fo stayed respectable and, in a more comic subplot, found the bubble lane. Jeff T kept digging, Wild Bill stayed in the pain wing, and the early new-blood noise from A.I. Dave and Rob F got drowned out by one very loud commissioner sequel.

📈 Movers

BostnMike

Went from hot to fully inconvenient. The page now looks like it was designed by his campaign staff.

Li-Fo

Still positive, still relevant, and still hanging around every serious conversation even when the spotlight moved elsewhere.

The Commissioner Narrative

Unfortunately for everyone else, it now has data support.

Jeff T

Another week, more entries, same complete absence of joy. The basement has become a furnished apartment.

Wild Bill

Still in the red, still looking for the version of this comeback tour that includes applause.

Opening-Night Hiro

What looked like a kingdom three weeks ago now reads like a pilot episode with no renewal guarantee.

BM

BostnMike didn’t just follow one stolen board with another good week. He followed it with a full administrative takeover. At this point the numbers are no longer humoring him — they’re enabling him, which is frankly more dangerous for the rest of us.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • BostnMike: from $105 to $180, with hits rising from 4 to 6 and the entire top shelf of the honors page suddenly reading like a résumé.
  • Li-Fo: still positive at $35 and now the room’s Bubble King, which is one way to stay relevant without controlling the story.
  • Jeff T: from -$120 to -$150, still hitless and still cashless — the purest misery lane on the league table.
  • Wild Bill: from -$90 to -$120, proving that early-season patience remains an expensive hobby.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • Opening-night Hiro is now officially a historical figure.
  • BostnMike took the whole board and will now be impossible to mute.
  • Li-Fo stayed respectable and still found time to pick up the bubble subplot.
  • Jeff T remains the room’s most reliable supplier of cautionary material.
  • Wild Bill is still searching for the profitable cut of this movie.
  • The June 14 newcomers are no longer the story. They’re just citizens in the new regime.
  • No new blood this week, just old problems getting promoted.
  • The relaunch hierarchy lasted about fifteen minutes before somebody rewrote it.
  • The spreadsheet has already developed a villain arc.
  • Three weeks in, the room is somehow already on its second era.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was June 21: opening-night Hiro officially faded into chapter one, BostnMike turned a heater into a regime, and the rest of the room got the unpleasant task of deciding whether this was temporary or the start of something worse. Either way, the early-season order is gone. This is the commish’s mess now.

weeks.title: The Week That Was
weeks.dek: The commish ran the table, new blood arrived loud, and the early hierarchy already looked a lot less settled than it did on opening night.
weeks.html:
Episode two did what good sequels are supposed to do: it messed up the neat little story from opening night. TLPT came back from hiatus with Hiro holding basically every crown in sight, and three weeks later, the table already looked like it had been broken into. The biggest thief? The commissioner himself.

“The commish stole the show.”

🎯 The Big Story

The cleanest compare-file story belonged to BostnMike. He went from a losing opener to outright board theft in one snapshot — profit jumping from -$30 to +$105, hits from 1 to 4, and the whole page suddenly reading like he had been right about himself all along. By June 14, he was sitting on Profit Leader, Hit King, Highest Profit, Most Hits, and Best Luck Index. That is not a bounce. That is a coup.

The part that makes it funnier is that the room had just spent opening night watching Hiro own everything. Now the belts were moving, the crowns were splitting, and the spreadsheet already looked like it regretted getting attached too early.

BM
Week’s Biggest Board Theft
BostnMike
Profit LeaderHit KingHighest ProfitBest Luck Index

This was also the league’s first real new-blood week. A.I. Dave showed up and immediately started acting like the model had been trained on everyone else’s mistakes. Rob F arrived and did the even ruder thing: he took the clutch stuff. That is how you know the league was truly back — it took almost no time at all for new people to start bothering the regulars.

Of course, not everyone enjoyed the sequel. Hiro came back to earth a bit, Wild Bill stayed in the pain lane, and Jeff T found the basement with the kind of commitment that should probably count as its own side event.

📈 Movers

BostnMike

The biggest swing on the page. From opening-night self-own to board owner in one jump.

A.I. Dave

Immediate impact, immediate credibility, and immediate ownership of the “serious player has entered the chat” lane.

Rob F

New blood, one entry, and already walking away with the clutch trophies. Very rude first impression.

Li-Fo

Didn’t own the headline this time, but stayed positive enough to remain part of every serious conversation.

Hiro

Still good, just no longer running the entire city. The opener king came back to earth fast.

Jeff T

From painful to worse. The basement now has assigned seating.

Wild Bill

Another rough line, another reminder that a comeback tour is not automatically a profitable one.

Opening Night Certainty

Gone. Completely gone. The league is already mutinying.

BM

BostnMike went from opening-night paperwork to outright board theft in one snapshot. Naturally, he now has exactly enough evidence to become intolerable and not quite enough to become dignified.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • BostnMike: from -$30 to +$105, with hits jumping from 1 to 4 — the biggest swing on the page.
  • A.I. Dave: first appearance, first cash, and already sitting on Power Leader, Top True Skill, and Best ROI.
  • Rob F: first appearance and immediate Clutch Leader, which is the statistical version of walking into the room and taking someone else’s chair.
  • Jeff T: from -$60 to -$120, still hitless, still cashless, now officially running a private basement operation.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • The commish ran the table and will now be impossible to reason with for at least one news cycle.
  • A.I. Dave’s first impression was less “hello” and more “move.”
  • Rob F walked in, grabbed the clutch hardware, and kept moving.
  • Hiro went from monarch to citizen in record time.
  • Li-Fo stayed respectable, which in this room is practically a diplomatic skill.
  • Jeff T found the basement and appears to have signed a lease.
  • Wild Bill remains available for further statistical hardship.
  • This was the first real sign that the post-hiatus board would not stay tidy for long.
  • No one should have trusted opening night as a long-term forecast.
  • The spreadsheet got interesting fast.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was June 14: the commish ran the table, new blood arrived loud, and the clean little opening-night hierarchy immediately got thrown into traffic. The league was not just back. It was already arguing with itself.

weeks.title: The Week That Was
weeks.dek: TLPT came back from hiatus, Hiro immediately grabbed the whole board, and the rest of the room spent opening night mostly funding the relaunch.
weeks.html:
TLPT came back from the dead, and it did not take long for the room to remember two things: the cards still hurt, and somebody was still going to own the night. After the long hiatus, the league reopened with all the usual optimism, self-delusion, and selective memory. Hiro responded by taking basically everything that wasn’t nailed down.

“TLPT is back, and Hiro took the whole board.”

🎯 The Big Story

Opening night belonged to Hiro. He finished as Profit Leader, Power Leader, Clutch Leader, Hit King, and even Bubble King, which is an impressively greedy way to restart a league. He also sat on Highest Profit, Best ROI, Most Hits, Most Cashes, Top True Skill, Best Clutch Index, and Best Luck Index. In other words, the hiatus ended and Hiro immediately started acting like he owned the resurrection.

Li-Fo was the only other player who really looked like he had read the assignment. A positive profit line, a cash, and a clean opener. Unfortunately for him, that still translated to “good night, wrong headline.”

HI
Opening Night Boss
Hiro
Profit LeaderHit KingBest ROITop True Skill

Elsewhere, the relaunch had a very familiar feel. Wild Bill and Jeff T both managed the sort of opening night that makes a player wonder if the hiatus had actually been a gift. BostnMike, naturally, found the extremely commissioner-coded lane of getting one hit and still losing money, which is a nice reminder that league leadership and personal success remain two different departments.

📈 Movers

Hiro

Owned the opener. Every major board worth checking somehow ended up with his name on it.

Li-Fo

A strong opening night and the only real supporting success story. Solid, profitable, and immediately overshadowed.

Wild Bill

Two entries, no hits, no cashes, and a -$60 reminder that comeback stories are not automatically assigned.

Jeff T

Same financial line as Wild Bill, same general pain, slightly different flavor of regret.

BM

BostnMike reopened the league the way only a commissioner could: one hit, no cash, negative profit, and just enough involvement to pretend the night had been productive. The league was back. The self-own was back faster.

🔢 Numbers That Matter

  • Hiro: +$115, 383.3% ROI, 2 hits, 1 cash, and basically every meaningful crown on the page.
  • Li-Fo: +$65 with a cash, which would have been a headline in most rooms. This was not most rooms.
  • Wild Bill / Jeff T: both finished at -$60, proving the relaunch had immediate donors.
  • BostnMike: one hit, -$30, and an opening-night line that already felt on brand.

🔥 Quick Hits

  • TLPT came back from hiatus and Hiro immediately started collecting trophies like lost luggage.
  • Li-Fo had a good night and still somehow ended up as the supporting cast.
  • Wild Bill and Jeff T helped fund the relaunch in the least ceremonial way possible.
  • BostnMike’s quote was “Play as tight as you want, Mike,” which history will note was not especially prophetic.
  • The room waited a long time to come back and learned nothing in the interim.
  • No new blood story here — just old habits stretching their legs.
  • The opener had one clear winner and several immediate cautionary examples.
  • The spreadsheet wasted no time getting judgmental again.
  • If this was the soft launch, the room may need harder helmets.
  • Opening night felt less like a restart and more like a resumption of services.
The Spreadsheet’s Final Word

🎬 Closing

So that was May 23: TLPT came back, Hiro took the whole board, Li-Fo kept it respectable, and the rest of the room mostly resumed its civic duty of making the winner look even better. The hiatus ended. The hierarchy introduced itself immediately.
